item.title: Plex wants you to pay $750 for everything Jellyfin can do for free
item.itunes:title: Plex wants you to pay $750 for everything Jellyfin can do for free
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When the Plex's Lifetime Pass originally launched, it was $75 — a price that a lot of folks got it for, and still talk about to this day. Fourteen years later, that price is about to become ten times the initial cost, making all Plex Pass users feel like early Bitcoin buyers. I myself got the Lifetime Pass at $120 during the decade-long period when the price didn't change, which is why I know just how useful the additional benefits of a Plex Pass can be.

However, as someone who has used almost every feature in the Plex Pass to enhance the base experience, it's impossible to deny the utility of those paid benefits. A Lifetime Plex Pass may be $250 today, but come July 2026, it'll set you back $750. Thankfully, Plex's closest rival, Jellyfin, has really come into its own over the years. Unless you have $750 to burn, all of Plex Pass' benefits can be added to your own self-hosted Jellyfin media server without spending a single cent.

Plex Pass really does have a number of benefits

These are features impossible to live without once you use them

While basic media hosting is free with Plex, a lot of the features you'd otherwise expect to have are locked behind the Plex Pass paywall. In fact, something as basic as remote viewing, where you can access your media server from outside your home's network, or let friends share your library, requires the Plex Pass. If you've ever binge-watched a TV series on online streaming networks like Netflix, the Skip Intro button becomes invaluable. However, on Plex, only users with the Plex Pass get to skip intros.

Even downloading your own movies, or using your own hardware for hardware-accelerated streaming are locked behind the Plex Pass.

Add premium music features, SDR-to-HDR tone mapping, restrictions based on family members, and even other great features like pre-roll trailers and clips for the full extended cinema-going experience, and the Lifetime Plex Pass begins looking like a must-have. Of course, regardless of whether it's $250 right now or $750 two months later, all of these features can still be enjoyed for free if you decide to use Jellyfin, instead.

Jellyfin is 90% of Plex, and the missing 10% doesn't matter to me

Free and open source over greedy subscriptions

61
By  Patrick Hearn

Jellyfin requires elbow grease, but not a single dollar

It's just as feature-packed, if not more

For the longest time, one of the biggest (and truest) arguments against Jellyfin has been the amount of research and effort it takes to self-host a media server using the tool. For the most part, the elbow grease required has decreased, but there are still a few hoops to jump through, like finding the right repository and adding the right plugins to your server. That being said, nothing beats free features, and every single Plex Pass feature has a Jellyfin plugin equivalent you can install and use.

My family might make the most out of my Lifetime Plex Pass, but I couldn't justify asking my friend to pay for another pass as they insisted on setting up their own media service. As such, we decided to go with a Jellyfin setup instead of Plex, and I was pleasantly surprised by just how far the open-source media server has come. In fact, considering just how "community-based" Jellyfin is, it's really helpful when users create entire repositories full of the best plugins that you can then install in succession, with as few clicks as possible.

Plex Pass Feature

Jellyfin Equivalent

Plugin/Method

Hardware-accelerated transcoding

Built into Jellyfin

Native support for Intel, AMD, and Nvidia

HDR to SDR tone mapping

Supported for free

Native tone mapping

Skip Intro

Yes

Intro Skipper plugin

Skip Credits

Yes

Intro Skipper / intro detection plugins

Offline downloads

Yes

Native mobile download support

Remote streaming

Yes

Built into Jellyfin

Library sharing with friends

Yes

Native multi-user support

Live TV + DVR

Yes

Native Live TV & DVR support

Pre-roll cinema trailers

Yes

Playback PreRoll plugin

Advanced metadata management

Yes

TMDb, AniDB, TVDB, OMDb plugins

Server dashboards & monitoring

Yes

Jellystat

Watch history sync

Yes

Trakt plugin

Plex and Jellyfin are no longer worlds apart

Plex's open-source rival is ready for prime time

There was a time when recommending Jellyfin to anyone other than diehard self-hosting enthusiasts felt irresponsible. The average user simply wasn't cut out for the legwork involved. You needed five different tutorials in five tabs, plugins required multiple prerequisite installations, and simply getting remote access working reliably took a full weekend. At the same time, Plex was polished, streamlined, and approachable, even for the least tech-savvy users. That's exactly why Plex became the default recommendation for years, despite its best features sitting behind the Plex Pass paywall.

Today, things couldn't be more different. The gap between Plex and Jellyfin is no longer a chasm because modern Jellyfin clients feel cleaner and plugin repositories are much easier to manage. Even the overall setup process has become significantly less intimidating than it was at the beginning of this decade. Bottom line: Jellyfin no longer feels like a compromise. When a free, community-driven media server can replicate nearly every premium Plex Pass feature without charging you $750 upfront, it's impossible not to question what exactly you're paying for anymore.

Jellyfin
iOS compatible
Yes
Android compatible
Yes
Desktop compatible
Yes

Jellyfin is one of the best Plex alternatives you can get, and that's thanks to its open-source nature and powerful set of features. There are apps for basically every platform and it's completely free to run your very own server.

See at Jellyfin
I switched to Emby, and it's way better than Plex and Jellyfin... in some ways

Plex to Jellyfin to Emby.

13
By  Rich Edmonds

Plex might want to make the Lifetime Pass unattainable

At the end of the day, Plex is genuinely still an excellent piece of software, and there's no denying it. It earned its reputation long before subscription fatigue became the norm, and for many longtime users, the convenience it offers is hard to walk away from. At this point, though, with the Plex Pass' biggest price hike ever right around the corner, even loyal users would begin to question whether convenience alone is worth such an aggressive asking price.

Subscribe to our newsletter for media-server insights

Unlock deeper coverage by subscribing to the newsletter: clear comparisons between Plex and Jellyfin, practical self-hosting setup tips, plugin recommendations, and cost breakdowns to help you choose the right media-server path.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Half the industry today agrees that this threefold price hike is just a way to make the Lifetime Plex Pass look so unobtainable that the annual and monthly subscriptions look better by comparison. After all, once the Lifetime Plex Pass costs as much as a PlayStation 5 Pro, paying a smaller recurring monthly fee will start to look acceptable.

Regardless of whether this move is intentional, it's a strategy that plenty of modern subscriptions have leaned into over the years, and Plex users are noticing it immediately. In the future, especially after July, getting into self-hosting media might become synonymous with Jellyfin, and I, for one, couldn't be more excited to see that day come.
item.description:
When the Plex's Lifetime Pass originally launched, it was $75 — a price that a lot of folks got it for, and still talk about to this day. Fourteen years later, that price is about to become ten times the initial cost, making all Plex Pass users feel like early Bitcoin buyers. I myself got the Lifetime Pass at $120 during the decade-long period when the price didn't change, which is why I know just how useful the additional benefits of a Plex Pass can be.

However, as someone who has used almost every feature in the Plex Pass to enhance the base experience, it's impossible to deny the utility of those paid benefits. A Lifetime Plex Pass may be $250 today, but come July 2026, it'll set you back $750. Thankfully, Plex's closest rival, Jellyfin, has really come into its own over the years. Unless you have $750 to burn, all of Plex Pass' benefits can be added to your own self-hosted Jellyfin media server without spending a single cent.

Plex Pass really does have a number of benefits

These are features impossible to live without once you use them

While basic media hosting is free with Plex, a lot of the features you'd otherwise expect to have are locked behind the Plex Pass paywall. In fact, something as basic as remote viewing, where you can access your media server from outside your home's network, or let friends share your library, requires the Plex Pass. If you've ever binge-watched a TV series on online streaming networks like Netflix, the Skip Intro button becomes invaluable. However, on Plex, only users with the Plex Pass get to skip intros.

Even downloading your own movies, or using your own hardware for hardware-accelerated streaming are locked behind the Plex Pass.

Add premium music features, SDR-to-HDR tone mapping, restrictions based on family members, and even other great features like pre-roll trailers and clips for the full extended cinema-going experience, and the Lifetime Plex Pass begins looking like a must-have. Of course, regardless of whether it's $250 right now or $750 two months later, all of these features can still be enjoyed for free if you decide to use Jellyfin, instead.

item.title: Your Wi-Fi antennas are pointing the wrong way
item.itunes:title: Your Wi-Fi antennas are pointing the wrong way
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
You could easily spend over $200 on a high-end Wi-Fi 6E or Wi-Fi 7 router. It looks like a mechanical spider. Naturally, you fold all six or eight external antennas outward at aggressive 45-degree angles, thinking you're creating an optimal geometric hemisphere of raw wireless power. In reality, by splaying those antennas out like a satellite dish, you're likely creating dead zones on the very same floor your router sits on. You've accidentally optimized your wireless network to blast data into your attic and your basement crawl space while starving your laptop of Wi-Fi in the main common areas of your home.

Wi-Fi antennas do not shoot signals out of their tips like flashlights. They radiate signals from their sides like an invisible donut. To maximize your signal, you need to align your antennas to match your home's layout, not what looks the coolest on your desk. Wi-Fi antennas are omnidirectional dipoles that radiate signals outward, perpendicular to the rod itself. Angling them diagonally doesn't push the signal further; it warps the transmission planes, throwing precious radio energy into the ceiling or floor rather than your devices.

5 router settings most people never touch that fixed my network problems

Lesser-known fixes for well-known problems

10
By  Chandraveer Mathur

The physics of your antenna

Don't ignore science

In order to understand how to position your Wi-Fi antennas, you must first understand the physics of the donut. Standard router antennas are omnidirectional dipole rods, meaning they radiate equal radio power in a 360° circle perpendicular to the rod's axis, forming a donut shape. This means that if an antenna is pointing straight up, it casts an invisible, massive donut-shaped shadow across the entire room. The entire donut is at the tip of the antenna.

If you then tilt that antenna to a 45° angle, you're tilting the donut. Now, half of your peak wireless energy is shooting up into the sky, and the other half is down onto the floorboards, creating a blind cone and a complete dead zone. Pointing an antenna directly at a device actually gives it the weakest possible connection. You want your devices to be perpendicular to the antennas.

It's also easy to fall into the cross-polarization trap. Polarization refers to the physical and spatial orientation of the electric field waves as they travel through space. When a standard router antenna stands perfectly vertical, it transmits an electric field that oscillates up and down. This is known as vertical polarization. In turn, an antenna laid flat on its side radiates an electric field that oscillates left to right, or horizontal polarization.

This is where the mainstream spider router setup completely falls apart. If you point every single antenna on your router straight up or tilt them all at the exact same uniform angle, you are flooding your house with data waves that are rigidly locked into a single polarization plane. In an office environment with desktop PCs and mounted smart TVs, that might work, as those devices sit stationary. However, your home network is dominated by the mobile factor. Your technology is constantly moving around your house and isn't on a fixed access point. You hold your smartphone vertically while scrolling social media, tilt it horizontally to watch a YouTube video, and toss it completely flat on a desk or coffee table while it streams music in the background. Every time you change the physical angle of your device, you tilt its internal antenna array.

Keep your home in mind

Different homes require different setups

So how are you meant to set up your router and its antennas? The correct way to do this is to match the antennas to your floor plan, meaning every single home will likely have a different setup. However, there are concrete, actionable configuration layouts tailored to your home's physical architecture.

If you have a single-story home, for example, a flat apartment or ranch house, then set all your antennas perfectly vertical, meaning they're pointing straight up at a 90° angle. This means it will keep the peak horizontal donut field entirely flat across your living space, maximizing the range across your walls.

For those in multi-story homes, your signal will likely need to travel upstairs or downstairs based on where the router is originally. Don't tilt everything! Instead, position one or two antennas perfectly vertical for the floor that the router is located on, and then flip one antenna completely horizontal, pointing flat, parallel to the floor or desk. Horizontal will radiate its signal vertically, punching data directly through the ceiling or floor to ensure you have a signal throughout your entire home.

If you have a multi-antenna array (more than four antennas on your router), go for the alternating 45° stagger. For a large open layout with multiple floors, split the difference. Leave the inner antennas vertical and tilt the outer antennas at alternating 45° angles in opposite directions. This creates a criss-cross pattern of vertical and horizontal polarization, ensuring a mobile device receives a clean signal no matter how you hold it.

There are a few exceptions to this rule. For example, if you have mesh systems. Modern cylindrical mesh nodes have no external rods. Their arrays are hard-coded into the plastic housing, and they're preconfigured vertically by engineers to optimize horizontal spread.

Stop spending hundreds on routers

Fix your antennas instead

Your router is a piece of networking infrastructure, not a sci-fi prop. Aggressive angles might look cool, but they defy the physics of radio wave propagation. Go and check your router right now and spend 30 seconds straightening out those rods to make your actual living layout. Open up a speed test app on the far side of your house and enjoy the free performance boost, which literally took you a couple of minutes to set up and configure.

TP-Link Dual-Band BE3600 Wi-Fi 7 Router

If you want a high speed router then the TP-Link Dual-Band BE3600 Wi-Fi 7 Router is a fantastic choice, with 4 antennas you can configure to your liking.

$120 at Amazon

This means that if an antenna is pointing straight up, it casts an invisible, massive donut-shaped shadow across the entire room. The entire donut is at the tip of the antenna.

If you then tilt that antenna to a 45° angle, you're tilting the donut. Now, half of your peak wireless energy is shooting up into the sky, and the other half is down onto the floorboards, creating a blind cone and a complete dead zone. Pointing an antenna directly at a device actually gives it the weakest possible connection. You want your devices to be perpendicular to the antennas.

It's also easy to fall into the cross-polarization trap. Polarization refers to the physical and spatial orientation of the electric field waves as they travel through space. When a standard router antenna stands perfectly vertical, it transmits an electric field that oscillates up and down. This is known as vertical polarization. In turn, an antenna laid flat on its side radiates an electric field that oscillates left to right, or horizontal polarization.

This is where the mainstream spider router setup completely falls apart. If you point every single antenna on your router straight up or tilt them all at the exact same uniform angle, you are flooding your house with data waves that are rigidly locked into a single polarization plane. In an office environment with desktop PCs and mounted smart TVs, that might work, as those devices sit stationary. However, your home network is dominated by the mobile factor. Your technology is constantly moving around your house and isn't on a fixed access point. You hold your smartphone vertically while scrolling social media, tilt it horizontally to watch a YouTube video, and toss it completely flat on a desk or coffee table while it streams music in the background. Every time you change the physical angle of your device, you tilt its internal antenna array.

Keep your home in mind

Different homes require different setups

So how are you meant to set up your router and its antennas? The correct way to do this is to match the antennas to your floor plan, meaning every single home will likely have a different setup. However, there are concrete, actionable configuration layouts tailored to your home's physical architecture.

If you have a single-story home, for example, a flat apartment or ranch house, then set all your antennas perfectly vertical, meaning they're pointing straight up at a 90° angle. This means it will keep the peak horizontal donut field entirely flat across your living space, maximizing the range across your walls.

For those in multi-story homes, your signal will likely need to travel upstairs or downstairs based on where the router is originally. Don't tilt everything! Instead, position one or two antennas perfectly vertical for the floor that the router is located on, and then flip one antenna completely horizontal, pointing flat, parallel to the floor or desk. Horizontal will radiate its signal vertically, punching data directly through the ceiling or floor to ensure you have a signal throughout your entire home.

If you have a multi-antenna array (more than four antennas on your router), go for the alternating 45° stagger. For a large open layout with multiple floors, split the difference. Leave the inner antennas vertical and tilt the outer antennas at alternating 45° angles in opposite directions. This creates a criss-cross pattern of vertical and horizontal polarization, ensuring a mobile device receives a clean signal no matter how you hold it.

There are a few exceptions to this rule. For example, if you have mesh systems. Modern cylindrical mesh nodes have no external rods. Their arrays are hard-coded into the plastic housing, and they're preconfigured vertically by engineers to optimize horizontal spread.

Stop spending hundreds on routers

Fix your antennas instead

Your router is a piece of networking infrastructure, not a sci-fi prop. Aggressive angles might look cool, but they defy the physics of radio wave propagation. Go and check your router right now and spend 30 seconds straightening out those rods to make your actual living layout. Open up a speed test app on the far side of your house and enjoy the free performance boost, which literally took you a couple of minutes to set up and configure.

TP-Link Dual-Band BE3600 Wi-Fi 7 Router

If you want a high speed router then the TP-Link Dual-Band BE3600 Wi-Fi 7 Router is a fantastic choice, with 4 antennas you can configure to your liking.

$120 at Amazon

item.title: I stopped using Google Drive and OneDrive so I could host my own files — and I love it
item.itunes:title: I stopped using Google Drive and OneDrive so I could host my own files — and I love it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Cloud storage is an incredibly useful technology that makes it easier than ever to keep copies of your files forever. But it also has a few downsides, including cost, the potential for someone to gain access to your account, or the simple fact that you're trusting a major corporation like Google or Microsoft to keep your data away from prying eyes. And when Microsoft has a list of file types that aren't allowed on OneDrive, you know the company has at least some access to that information.

Recently, I got into the rabbit hole of self-hosting, and Nextcloud and Immich have proven to the perfect solution for hosting my files, keeping them safe, private, and allowing me to share them with the people that matter.

Why I was done with cloud storage

Finding common ground was tough

The biggest reason I personally wanted to move away from cloud services was the cost. I was done with paying $7 per month for OneDrive a few months ago, but I was nearing the 40GB limit on my free account (I've used OneDrive for a long time so I have some loyalty bonuses).

Meanwhile, my wife has always used Google Drive and Photos, so we were also split across platforms and neither of us wanted to concede. I've never been a fan of Google services — not necessarily for any rational reason, but I refused to switch — and she had similar feelings about Microsoft.

Paying for two cloud services made no sense, and on top of that, these services cost a lot more than what we really wanted to spend. Sure, $10 per month for 2TB isn't a bad deal by any means, but we also wouldn't be using anywhere close to that maximum capacity. So we were in limbo for a long while until the idea of self-hosting came along.

Self-hosting vs. cloud storage: Which one really protects your data?

Between convenience and data control, both come with trade-offs

By  Karandeep Singh

Nextcloud is perfect for file management

Everything is backed up and works as expected

The first service I wanted to self-host was Nextcloud, which is a fairly beat-for-beat replacement for OneDrive or Google Drive. The big difference is, of course, everything is stored on your own private server and you have total control over your data.

As a replacement for OneDrive and Google Drive, Nextcloud is everything I could want it to be and then some. At its core, it's a simple file storage and management tool, and it works perfectly as such. There isn't much that can go wrong here, but you can create folders, subfolders, and whatever you need to organize your files in the way you prefer, all while keeping them safe and sound on your own server. Nextcloud can also open PDF files out of the box, and it comes with a calendar, and even an email client, though I don't really use the latter.

Nextcloud allows you to create multiple users, too, so I could have my own account while my wife has one for herself, and our files are completely separate. But we can also share files with each other easily. It's even possible to share files with users outside our home network, though we haven't really touched this. I rarely ever share files with outsiders, and if I do, it's for work, where I need to use work resources anyway.

What's even better is that Nextcloud has a desktop app that can sync easily just like OneDrive. You just install Nextcloud, and your files become available in File Explorer, including the ability to download them to store locally on demand. This feature doesn't work on Mac, so I have to choose between downloading them all or not having them visible, but still, it works fairly well.

6 ways Nextcloud beats Microsoft 365 entirely for free

Nextcloud is the ultimate free Microsoft 365 alternative

10
By  Parth Shah

I can even edit Office files

Nextcloud Office is a thing, too

One of the best things about Nextcloud is its extensibility. Nextcloud is an ecosystem in itself, with the ability to install apps such as Nextcloud Tasks, Passwords, and most importantly, Nextcloud Office. While I've already talked about Nextcloud Tasks before, Office is the most important part for me, and you can set it up in a couple of ways.

Nextcloud Office relies on Collabora Office, an Office suite based on LibreOffice that's made for online collaboration. To use Nextcloud Office, you need to set up a Collabora Office Document Editing (CODE) server, and you can either set up a separate instance on your NAS, or install the built-in CODE server in Nextcloud, which has slightly worse performance but makes things easier.

I went with the latter for simplicity, but it gets the job done just fine for the most part. I don't edit Office files very frequently, and most of the time, I'd rather work locally anyway, but it's nice to have this built-in feature that lets me open these Office files on any device to take a quick look or make a simple edit every now and then. I am considering setting up a standalone server, though, because I've noticed some performance hiccups when editing large Excel files.

This free self-hosted tool lets me sync my to-do lists across devices

Staying in sync while staying private

By  João Carrasqueira

Immich is everything I need in a media storage service

Google Photos, but all mine

I could have a centralized service for storing files and photos, and Nextcloud does offer a useful app called Memories that offers a nice photo management experience. But I've heard a lot of good things about Immich, so I wanted to try setting this up instead, and I love it, too.

Everything about Immich is made to look and feel like Google Photos, bit this time, everything is stored on my own server and on my own terms. Photos and videos are automatically sorted in chronological order, and I can easily find memories from specific times in my life. I can also create albums for specific memories, such as trips and special events, all of which are also displayed chronologically.

What's more, despite running entirely locally, Immich also has AI features similar to Google Photos, so it can identify people in photos and make it easy to find pictures with specific objects or people.

Much like Nextcloud, Immich supports multiple user accounts, so my wife and I have separate storage, but I can share albums with her to include memories of our time together. And again, it's possible to share photos with users outside of our network, but this isn't something I've felt the need to do.

5 reasons Immich is better than Google Photos and iCloud

Compelling reasons to retain your privacy and self-host your photos and videos with Immich

4
By  Ruby Helyer

Secure and remotely accessible

Reverse proxies and VPNs come in handy

Despite all of these files being stored on a server I have at home, I can still access all of them remotely when I'm outside. I've set up Tailscale to allow me to remotely connect to my home server using a tunneled connection, meaning the traffic sent between the server and my device can't be intercepted, and the connection is completely secure.

Deals

Storage & Networking Deals to Cut Home-Server Costs

Find deals and discounts on storage and networking gear to build or upgrade a self-hosted setup. Save on NAS units, hard drives, SSDs, routers, switches, backup subscriptions, and accessories—shop offers to lower your long-term hosting costs.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

Even better, I also bought and set up a custom domain, so I can access these services even more easily with custom URLs set up in the Nginx Proxy Manager. This isn't mandatory, but it makes things almost as easy as a typical cloud service, so I don't have to miss the convenience of a typical cloud service.

That's not to say I don't use the cloud at all, though. I've set up a Backblaze account so that my TrueNAS server backs up my Nextcloud and Immich instances automatically on a daily basis. This gives me peace of mind while lowering the risk of exposing my data to strangers or companies that may use that data for who knows what. Plus, Backblaze is a lot cheaper than something like OneDrive or Google Drive.

Without even owning a domain, I enabled HTTPS to secure my self-hosted apps with Tailscale - here's how you can do it too

I grabbed free TLS certificates from Tailscale to enable HTTPS for the self-hosted apps in my home lab.

2
By  Samir Makwana

Nextcloud and Immich made self-hosting worth it

My self-hosting journey has led me to install a few different services I love, but Nextcloud and Immich alone have made the trouble of learning about self-hosting worth it. I love not having to depend on a cloud service where I'm not in full control, and I love that I can still access my files on any device, from anywhere.

It's only been a few weeks, but I already can't imagine going back to relying on typical cloud services for storing my files. I'm in control and I can change anything I want about how these files are stored and made accessible. It's a perfect solution.

  • Nextcloud
    OS
    Windows, macOS, Linux
    Key highlights
    Self-hosted, open source
    See at Nextcloud
  • Immich
    Key highlights
    Self-hosted
    iOS compatible
    Yes
    See at Immich

item.title: Windows 11's new Start menu does things the old one never could — it actually saves time
item.itunes:title: Windows 11's new Start menu does things the old one never could — it actually saves time
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Good grief and good riddance, the old Windows 11 Start menu is gone. It’s something I don’t miss, and I hope it never comes back. The new Start menu has what I need, where I need it. What’s even better is that the redesigned Start menu showed up for me after KB5068861 in November, and Microsoft confirmed on May 15 that more controls are on their way. Microsoft said it wanted the Start menu to feel personal and calm, but I just care that it feels faster. It’s doing more, and I’m doing less to get my stuff done.

I stopped alt-tabbing every time my phone buzzed

The Start menu shows me my texts, calls, and files right there

The phone section in the new Windows Start menu is one of the things I like most about it. If I get a call or text message, I no longer have to reach for my phone since I can simply click the Start button to access it. For example, if I get a text message, the Phone Link panel of the Start menu will show a blue dot indicating I received one. I also have the option to access my phone's pictures without taking my eyes off the screen.

The best part is that I can send files from my computer to my phone, which is great when I need to keep working on my way out. I can either send an image or a doc, and now I can have the file in two places. So, if the power goes out and my laptop’s battery dies, I can use my phone to keep reviewing the file. I did run into an issue where I had to try to send a file twice, but I can’t say it surprised me.

My forgotten apps are easier to find because Start groups them for me

Sections like Social and Productivity put apps like WhatsApp back in plain sight

I love it when a layout makes an app or feature easier to find. The new Windows Start menu shows me my apps in three different ways: Category, List, and Grid. But I always keep them organized in my category since that makes a new app easier to find when I can’t remember its name. The Start menu organizes apps into categories such as Social, Productivity, Utilities & Tools, Developer Tools, Entertainment, Accessibility, and Information & Reading.

The Start menu was not the first place I would go to find my apps since I always have them in the Taskbar. However, when I needed to open the Start menu for whatever reason, I would see an app I had forgotten I had even installed, which would remind me to either uninstall it or test it out. That makes Start useful even when I’m not opening it to open an app.

The Start menu finally makes better use of my bigger screen

My monitor shows more apps without making the menu feel crowded

I’ve been using my Samsung monitor for a long time, and I never really noticed the difference between the Start menu on my laptop and the one on my external monitor. I mean, who actually stops to count how many icons are in each row? I would just open the Start menu, look for the app I needed, and open it. If I didn’t find it, I would search for it.

On my smaller 14-inch ASUS Zenbook display, I would only see 6 columns of pinned apps, 4 recommendations, and 3 columns of categories. But when I switched to my bigger monitor, the Start menu showed 8 columns of pinned apps, 6 recommendations, and 4 columns of categories. I can see more apps right away, which means fewer trips to the search bar. I have to scroll on my laptop because fewer apps fit on the screen, but that's not the case on my bigger monitor. It’s nice to see that the Start menu adjusts to my screen size.

The new Start menu is still missing a key option

Windows 10 users could resize it, but Windows 11 users can’t?

The new Start menu is better than the previous one, but I don’t like how much space it takes up. On my bigger monitor, it’s not that annoying because I have room to spare, but when I have to work without it, and only use my 14-inch ASUS Zenbook display, the space it hogs is just too much. There are times when it feels like I’m kind of using Windows 8 with the space it takes up.

What’s missing is the option to manually resize it myself. At least with Windows 10, I had the option of resizing it, but not with Windows 11. I only had to drag the border to resize it. I see the benefit of automatic sizing, but it doesn’t give me the size I want. Not having an option to resize it makes it feel like a step in the wrong direction.

Windows 11 is getting the feature I’ve been waiting for

The Small and Large options should give me more control

There is hope after all! On May 15, Microsoft confirmed it’s testing Start menu size settings with Windows Insiders, which should eventually let me choose between Small and Large layouts. That means my 14-inch laptop and my larger monitor can automatically show different Start layouts. Once the setting arrives, I should be able to choose the size that works better for me and not just accept whatever Windows picks for me.

Subscribe for hands-on Windows Start menu coverage

Want deeper context and practical how-tos? Subscribing to the newsletter gives focused, practical coverage of Start menu changes and broader Windows UI tweaks—clear explainers and settings walkthroughs to help you adopt new features with confidence.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

That’s the part I’m most excited about. I’m happy with everything else, such as the Phone Link panel, and how it adjusts to the monitor I’m using. When Microsoft releases these Start menu size controls, I’m going to be one happy camper.

The new design is what I need, and it’s going to get even better

I don’t want the old Start menu to ever come back. I’m good with the new one and looking forward to the announced changes. It’s a good improvement, since I can access my phone via the Phone Link panel, and when I’m using a larger monitor, I can automatically see more app options.

Windows 11 Pro

Windows 11 Pro is Microsoft's premium desktop operating system designed for business and hybrid work, layering, and AI-driven workflow.

See at Amazon See at Best Buy See at Microsoft
item.description:
Good grief and good riddance, the old Windows 11 Start menu is gone. It’s something I don’t miss, and I hope it never comes back. The new Start menu has what I need, where I need it. What’s even better is that the redesigned Start menu showed up for me after KB5068861 in November, and Microsoft confirmed on May 15 that more controls are on their way. Microsoft said it wanted the Start menu to feel personal and calm, but I just care that it feels faster. It’s doing more, and I’m doing less to get my stuff done.

I stopped alt-tabbing every time my phone buzzed

The Start menu shows me my texts, calls, and files right there

The phone section in the new Windows Start menu is one of the things I like most about it. If I get a call or text message, I no longer have to reach for my phone since I can simply click the Start button to access it. For example, if I get a text message, the Phone Link panel of the Start menu will show a blue dot indicating I received one. I also have the option to access my phone's pictures without taking my eyes off the screen.

The best part is that I can send files from my computer to my phone, which is great when I need to keep working on my way out. I can either send an image or a doc, and now I can have the file in two places. So, if the power goes out and my laptop’s battery dies, I can use my phone to keep reviewing the file. I did run into an issue where I had to try to send a file twice, but I can’t say it surprised me.

My forgotten apps are easier to find because Start groups them for me

Sections like Social and Productivity put apps like WhatsApp back in plain sight

I love it when a layout makes an app or feature easier to find. The new Windows Start menu shows me my apps in three different ways: Category, List, and Grid.
